title: Letter to a forgotten writer
summary: Marko writes an open letter to a Polish author who died more than eighty years ago. Four people in his city meet once a month to read the author out loud.
body:
Dear Bruno Schulz,

I do not know if these letters reach you. You died eighty-three years ago, in 1942. You were a teacher and a writer in a small Polish town. You wrote two books. The Nazis killed you in the street. Most of your stories are lost.

I am writing because I have just read your one surviving book. A friend gave it to me last spring. The book is small. The sentences are strange. They are full of light and dust at the same time. You wrote about your father, your shop, your street. They are very ordinary things. But you made them feel like dreams.

I want to tell you something. Outside Poland, almost no one knows your name now. In my country, your book is not in the shops. But four people in my city have read it. We have a small group. We meet once a month in a café. We read your sentences out loud.

I think you would like that. Four people in a café in a city you never saw, with your book on the table.

I am sorry the world forgot you for a long time. We did not know what we had. Some readers are slow.

Now we know.

With thanks,
Marko

grammarNotes.title: Past simple with 'ago' and exact years
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng quá khứ đơn để nói chuyện đã hoàn tất hẳn. Hai cách định vị thời gian thường gặp: (1) 'ago' — đứng sau khoảng thời gian, đếm ngược từ hiện tại: 'You died eighty-three years ago.' (2) năm cụ thể với 'in': 'in 1942', 'in 2010'. Câu thường gặp có cả hai: 'You died eighty-three years ago, in 1942.' Động từ bất quy tắc đổi hình: die → died, write → wrote, kill → killed, give → gave, make → made.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past simple for finished actions. Two common time markers: (1) 'ago' goes after a length of time and counts back from now — 'You died eighty-three years ago.' (2) Specific years use 'in': 'in 1942.' You can pair them: 'eighty-three years ago, in 1942.' Watch irregular verbs: write → wrote, give → gave, make → made.

translationVi:
Kính gửi ông Bruno Schulz,

Tôi không biết những lá thư này có đến tay ông không. Ông mất tám mươi ba năm trước, năm 1942. Ông từng là giáo viên và nhà văn ở một thị trấn nhỏ Ba Lan. Ông viết hai cuốn sách. Quân phát-xít bắn chết ông ngoài đường. Phần lớn truyện của ông bị thất lạc.

Tôi viết vì tôi vừa đọc xong cuốn sách duy nhất còn lại của ông. Một người bạn đưa cho tôi mùa xuân năm ngoái. Cuốn sách nhỏ. Câu chữ kỳ lạ. Đầy ánh sáng và bụi cùng một lúc. Ông viết về cha ông, cửa hàng ông, con phố ông. Toàn những điều rất bình thường. Nhưng ông khiến chúng giống như những giấc mơ.

Tôi muốn nói với ông một điều. Bên ngoài Ba Lan, gần như không ai biết tên ông nữa. Ở nước tôi, sách của ông không có trong tiệm. Nhưng bốn người trong thành phố tôi đã đọc nó. Chúng tôi có một nhóm nhỏ. Mỗi tháng một lần, chúng tôi gặp nhau trong một quán cà phê. Chúng tôi đọc to những câu của ông.

Tôi nghĩ ông sẽ thích điều đó. Bốn người trong một quán cà phê tại một thành phố ông chưa từng thấy, có sách của ông trên bàn.

Tôi xin lỗi vì thế giới đã quên ông suốt một thời gian dài. Chúng tôi không biết mình đang có gì. Có những người đọc chậm.

Giờ chúng tôi biết.

Với lòng biết ơn,
Marko.

options: Bruno Schulz, a Polish writer who died in 1942
questions.explanation: 'Dear Bruno Schulz. You died eighty-three years ago, in 1942.'

questions.prompt: What was Bruno Schulz's job, besides writing?
questions.explanation: 'You were a teacher and a writer in a small Polish town.'

options: The Nazis killed him in the street
questions.explanation: 'The Nazis killed you in the street.'

questions.prompt: How did Marko get the book?
options: He bought it in a shop.
A friend gave it to him last spring.
He found it in the street.
questions.explanation: 'A friend gave it to me last spring.'

questions.prompt: How does Marko describe Bruno Schulz's sentences?
options: Strange — full of light and dust at the same time.
questions.explanation: 'The sentences are strange. They are full of light and dust at the same time.'

questions.prompt: What did Bruno Schulz write about?
options: His father, his shop, his street — ordinary things made to feel like dreams
questions.explanation: 'You wrote about your father, your shop, your street. They are very ordinary things. But you made them feel like dreams.'

questions.prompt: How many people in Marko's city have read the book?
questions.explanation: 'Four people in my city have read it.'

questions.prompt: What does the small group do?
options: They meet once a month in a café and read his sentences out loud.
They translate his work into many languages.
They protest at his old school.
questions.explanation: 'We meet once a month in a café. We read your sentences out loud.'

questions.prompt: What does Marko mean by 'Some readers are slow'?
options: Some people read books at a low speed.
The world took a long time to notice Bruno Schulz — and that is partly the readers' fault.
Old books take years to read.
Marko reads more slowly than the others in the group.
questions.explanation: He says the world 'forgot you for a long time. We did not know what we had.'
questions.hint: Look at the line just before it.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Speaking aloud to a forgotten writer — even by readers who arrived late — is one small way of returning what was taken.
Polish writers are the best in the world.
Bookshops should sell only old books.
questions.explanation: Marko writes a public 'thank you' on behalf of late readers — modest, but real.
questions.hint: Think about what the letter is trying to give back to the writer.

blanks.explanation: Most of his stories are lost.
A friend gave it to him last spring.
Ordinary things made to feel like dreams.
They meet in a café once a month.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marko writes a letter that the dead writer will never read, and gathers four readers in a café around it. Is there someone — a writer, a teacher, a relative — you would write to today even though they cannot answer? What would change for you, even if nothing changed for them?
